facts: The long-wheelbase, fifth-generation Sportage for United States and Canada was revealed in October 2021 as a 2023 model year.
It is locally produced in West Point, Georgia instead of imported from South Korea.
The hybrid version is equipped with the T-GDi engine and a 44.2kW electric motor combined with a 1.49 kWh battery that also delivers 226hp (229PS; 169kW).
It has an estimated range of 500mi (800km) and can be optioned with front-wheel drive or all-wheel drive with central differential lock.
The fifth-generation Sportage was unveiled on 8 June 2021, with specifications revealed in July 2021.
Based on the brand’s latest N3 platform, Kia developed two versions of the Sportage with different body length and wheelbase depending on the market.
Hybrid and plug-in hybrid models will also feature an E-Handling system.
For the European market, Kia offers several range of drivetrains that includes plug-in hybrid, hybrid, mild hybrid, and diesel engine.
The PHEV version features a 1.6-litre four-cylinder T-GDi engine and a 66.9kW permanent magnet traction electric motor with a 13.8kWh lithium-ion battery.
The whole system produces 261hp (265PS; 195kW).
The hybrid model with same T-GDi engine and a 44.2kW electric motor paired with a 1.49kWh battery and produces 226hp (229PS; 169kW).
The mild-hybrid model also uses the 1.6-litre T-GDi engine, which produces either 147hp (149PS; 110kW) or 177hp (179PS; 132kW).
A 7-speed dual-clutch transmission is available for the 1.6-litre while a 6-speed manual transmission is standard for all variants.
All European models are equipped with Stop-and-Go technology.

The fifth-generation Sportage in the short-wheelbase version was launched in Brazil on 1 July 2022, with two trim levels: EX and EX Prestige; it is powered by a 1.6 T-GDi 48V turbocharged petrol mild hybrid.
In February 2024, the Hybrid version based on the SXL trim was added to the range in Mexico.
The facelifted Sportage was released in North America in May 2025.
In March 2024, the FWD Hybrid version was added to the range for the SX and GT Line trim levels in Australia.
In January 2024, the Hybrid version was added to the range with two variants available in New Zealand.
The Smartstream G1.6 T-GDi is a 1,598cc turbocharged I4 producing 180PS (132kW; 178hp) @ 5,500rpm with 27kg⋅m (265N⋅m; 195lbf⋅ft) torque available from 1,500–4,000rpm.
The Smartstream G2.0 MPi is a 1,999cc I4 producing 152PS (112kW; 150hp) or 156PS (115kW; 154hp) @ 6,200rpm with 19.6kg⋅m (192N⋅m; 142lbf⋅ft) torque.
The Smartstream G2.0 T-GDi is a 1,975cc I4 producing 236PS (174kW; 233hp) @ 6,000rpm with 36.0kg⋅m (353N⋅m; 260lbf⋅ft) torque available from 1,500-4,000 rpm.
The Smartstream G2.5 GDi is a 2,497cc I4 producing 190PS (140kW; 187hp) @ 6,100rpm with 25kg⋅m (245N⋅m; 181lbf⋅ft) torque.
The Smartstream G1.6 T-GDi 48V mild hybrid is a 1,598cc turbocharged I4 producing 150PS (110kW; 148hp) @ 5,500rpm with 25.5kg⋅m (250N⋅m; 184lbf⋅ft) torque.
The Smartstream G1.6 T-GDi Hybrid produces 230PS (169kW; 227hp) @ 5,500rpm with 35.7kg⋅m (350N⋅m; 258lbf⋅ft) torque available from 1,500–4,500rpm.
The Smartstream G1.6 T-GDi Plug-in Hybrid produces 265PS (195kW; 261hp) @ 5,500rpm.
The Smartstream L2.0 LPi is a 1,999cc I4 producing 146PS (107kW; 144hp) @ 6,000rpm with 19.5kg⋅m (191N⋅m; 141lbf⋅ft) torque.

The Smartstream D1.6 CRDi is a 1,598cc turbocharged I4 producing 115PS (85kW; 113hp) or 136PS (100kW; 134hp) @ 4,000rpm with torque ranging from 28.6kg⋅m (280N⋅m; 207lbf⋅ft) to 32.6kg⋅m (320N⋅m; 236lbf⋅ft).
The Smartstream D2.0 CRDi is a 1,998cc turbocharged I4 producing 186PS (137kW; 183hp) @ 4,000rpm with 42.5kg⋅m (417N⋅m; 307lbf⋅ft) torque available from 2,000–2,750rpm.
The Sportage was Kia's best selling model worldwide in 2016, overtaking the Rio.
The Biden administration is betting on renewing the American industrial machine to speed progress toward decarbonizing the U.S. electric grid by 2035.
The White House aims to use clean electricity to power a mass transition to electric vehicles to tackle transportation greenhouse gases.
Incentives in the Inflation Reduction Act spurred the emergence of a new Battery Belt spanning Michigan, Indiana, Ohio, Kentucky, Tennessee, Georgia, and the Carolinas.
Former Michigan Governor Jennifer Granholm picked battery and EV production as a strategic growth industry over a decade ago to help Michigan recover from the Great Recession.
Georgia leads the country in post-Inflation Reduction Act clean energy factory investments with over $17 billion, according to a Canary Media analysis.
South Carolina ranks second with $9.3 billion in clean energy factory investments, followed by Michigan with $8.1 billion.
The Southeast built an industrial foundation in chemicals and advanced manufacturing that now supports EV manufacturing growth.
Matt Kisber, former economic development lead for Tennessee Governor Phil Bredesen, helped recruit Volkswagen to build its Chattanooga plant.
Lithium mining history dates back to the Foote Mineral Company opening the Kings Mountain Mine in 1938 in the Carolina Tin-Spodumene Belt across Cleveland and Gaston counties.
Lithium from the Carolina Tin-Spodumene Belt was used for ceramics, pharmaceuticals, and Manhattan Project thermonuclear research during the Cold War.
The Kings Mountain lithium mine shut down in 1988 due to corporate cost-cutting and overseas manufacturing trends.
Livent's Hallman-Beam Mine near Bessemer City shuttered in 1996.
Albemarle, which now owns the Kings Mountain mine, is conducting economic-feasibility studies to revive the dormant site.
Newcomer Piedmont Lithium hopes to open a new mine in the area, though its initial public outreach campaign faced opposition over concerns about mining explosions and water well destabilization.
Global lithium suppliers Albemarle and Livent still process lithium near Charlotte, North Carolina.
If regional mines reopen, they could close the loop on localized EV production by mining, refining, battery fabrication, vehicle assembly, and recycling within a couple hundred miles.

Lithium markets expert Joe Lowry stated that reopening mines would complement hard-rock mining in Canada and extraction from brines and clay formations elsewhere in the U.S. to create a diversified North American supply chain.
Energy Secretary Jennifer Granholm emphasized that while the U.S. should extract critical minerals domestically, new mining requires environmental reforms and respect for tribal communities.

Former Charlotte Mayor Jennifer Roberts noted that state regulators often lack resources to police industrial polluters but highlighted Vulcan Materials' community engagement practices as a positive model.
South Korea's SK Corporation opened a $2.6 billion battery factory in Commerce, Georgia, in early 2022 before the Inflation Reduction Act passed.
The SK Battery America factory ships batteries to Volkswagen's electric ID.4 production site in Chattanooga and supplies Ford's F-150 Lightning.
The facility produces enough batteries to power 300,000 EVs annually according to company statements.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp praised SK for hitting employment targets two years ahead of schedule, with 2,600 employees already and a target of 3,000 by year-end.
Norwegian company Freyr is fast-tracking a second factory in Coweta County, southwest of Atlanta, to leverage Inflation Reduction Act benefits.
Freyr is making a $2.6 billion investment in Georgia in partnership with Koch Industries.
Freyr expects to produce 2.5 gigawatt-hours of batteries in 2025, ramping to 38 gigawatt-hours shortly thereafter.
The Freyr plant will employ more than 700 people and licensed battery technology from MIT spinout 24M.
SK Corporation is teaming up with Hyundai to build a $5 billion factory in northern Georgia.
SK Corporation is also partnering with Ford to build $5.7 billion factories near Memphis and in southern Kentucky.
Georgia appointed an economic development lead specifically for South Korea in 2006, with Yoonie Kim facilitating $11.5 billion of Korean investment in the state in 2022 alone.
The Kemp administration utilizes tax incentives and the state-funded Quick Start worker training program to attract EV employers.
Volkswagen's Chattanooga factory received its global board in July 2022 to commemorate the start of electric vehicle production.

The VW ID.4 is largely sourced from within the U.S., with steel from Alabama and Ohio, interior parts from Indiana and South Carolina, electronics from Kentucky and North Carolina, and batteries from SK's Georgia base.
Volkswagen ramped up ID.4 assembly last year by hiring 1,000 new employees and adding a third shift, with another 500 hires planned for the following year.
Tennessee is home to approximately 900 auto parts suppliers, a network that increasingly includes batteries to reduce global shipping disruption risks.
Roughly half the U.S. population lives within 650 miles of Nashville, providing Tennessee with a logistical advantage for distributing finished vehicles.
Memphis serves as a historic logistics hub halfway up the Mississippi River and is a global air freight center due to FedEx.
Ford is building its combined battery and EV mega-plant, BlueOval City, in Memphis.
General Motors manufactures electric Cadillacs at a facility in Spring Hill, Tennessee.
BMW added EV production to its plant in Spartanburg, South Carolina.
Hyundai and Kia are building new manufacturing sites in Georgia.
Rivian's proposed site outside Atlanta faced legal challenges as the state's incentive package was tied up in court by opposing residents.
Central North Carolina attracted new factories from Toyota and Vietnamese firm VinFast.
The Southeastern region is solidly right to work, a policy used to weaken union power, though Tennessee recently passed a constitutional amendment to perpetuate this status.
United Auto Workers President Shawn Fain has advocated for ensuring union representation at new EV plants in the region.
Ascend Elements opened the first operational battery recycler in the Southeastern Battery Belt in Covington, Georgia, this spring.
Ascend Elements uses a two-story-tall wet shredder to process used batteries and scrap materials from nearby factories like SK's.
The recycling facility separates aluminum, copper, magnetic bits, and black mass powder containing electrode metals by the 1,000-kilogram sack.
Ascend Elements is breaking ground on a $1 billion factory in southern Kentucky to use its hydro-to-cathode technique for pulling exact element ratios for battery makers.
Ascend Elements announced a $1 billion deal to supply an unnamed U.S. battery manufacturer.
Redwood Materials, founded by Tesla co-founder JB Straubel, is building a $3.5 billion recycling plant near Charleston, South Carolina.
By 2025, Redwood Materials plans to produce 100 gigawatt-hours per year of cathode active materials and anode foil across its U.S. operations, supplying approximately 1 million electric vehicles.
Redwood Materials spokesperson Daniel Zotos stated that localizing anode and cathode production lowers manufacturing environmental footprints, mitigates supply-chain risks, and drives down EV costs.
Recyclers acknowledge that domestic recycling will not meet soaring battery demand for at least the next decade, requiring fresh mining to sustain EV production.
Representative Sean Casten (D-Illinois) emphasized the need to keep high-value-add manufacturing in America to maintain a competitive advantage.
The Southeastern Battery Belt has become indispensable to the national quest to clean up transportation-related emissions by locking in value-added EV supply chain activities.

facts: Hyundai Motor Company is a South Korean multinational automotive manufacturer headquartered in Seoul, South Korea.
The company owns 33.88 percent of Kia Corporation and owns the Genesis luxury car subsidiary, with all three brands making up the Hyundai Motor Group.
In May 2023, Hyundai established a joint venture with LG Energy Solution to manufacture electric vehicle batteries in the US.
In October 2024, Hyundai Motor signed a 610-gigawatt-hour (GWh) power purchase agreement (PPA) with SK E&S and other partners for its renewable energy transition efforts.

In November 2024, Hyundai announced that Global Chief Operating Officer Jose Munoz would become global co-CEO beginning January 1, 2025, a promotion credited to his strategies in pushing record sales in the North America region.
Hyundai incorporated a new manufacturing facility, Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama, in April 2002.
The Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama plant is located in Montgomery, Alabama, was completed during 2004 at a cost of $1.7 billion, and started production in May 2005.
The Montgomery, Alabama plant employed more than 3,000 workers in 2012.
Hyundai operates research and development centers in South Korea, Germany, the United States (California), Japan, India, and China.
Hyundai established its Design Center in Fountain Valley, California in 1990, which moved to a new $30 million facility in Irvine, California in 2003.

The Hyundai America Technical Center, Inc., responsible for all engineering activities in the US for Hyundai, moved to a new 200,000-square-foot, $117 million headquarters in Superior Township, Michigan in 2005.
In 2004, the Hyundai America Technical Center completed construction of its Hyundai/Kia proving ground in California City, California, a 4,300-acre facility built at a cost of $50 million.
Hyundai established Supernal, an urban air mobility (UAM) subsidiary, in the United States in 2020.
On November 8, 2023, Hyundai announced plans to build a plant in the US to produce flying taxis under its Supernal subsidiary.
As of February 2025, a full-scale technology demonstrator for the Supernal flying taxi was undergoing ground testing.
The Supernal headquarters is located in Washington, D.C., with an R&D facility in Fremont, California, and engineering headquarters in Irvine, California.
Hyundai began selling cars in the United States on February 20, 1986, with a single model, the Hyundai Excel.
In its first year of business in the United States in 1986, Hyundai set a record for selling the most automobiles compared to any other car brand, with total sales of 168,882.
The company added free maintenance for the first 2 years or 24,000 miles for all its new cars sold in the United States, starting with the 1992 model year.
Hyundai added a 10-year or 100,000-mile (160,000km) powertrain warranty to its vehicles sold in the United States.
In 2004, Hyundai tied with Honda for initial brand quality in a J.D. Power and Associates survey for having 102 problems per 1000 vehicles.
The company placed third overall in J.D. Power's 2006 Initial Quality Survey, behind only Porsche and Lexus.
In 2009, the Hyundai Genesis luxury sedan was named 2009 North American Car of the Year.
In January 2012, the Hyundai Elantra was named the North American Car of the Year at the North American International Auto Show, having sold more than 200,000 cars since its redesigned debut.
As of November 2024, Hyundai is the world's third-largest carmaker in terms of production, behind competitors Toyota and Volkswagen.
In 2024, Hyundai produced 4,146,335 vehicles.
The company employs approximately 75,000 people worldwide.

TechnipFMC is a leading technology provider to the traditional and new energies industry delivering fully integrated projects, products, and services.
The company operates within three New Energy pillars: Offshore Hydrogen, Green House Gas removal, and Floating Renewables.
TechnipFMC generates hydrogen offshore by electrolyzing seawater using renewable power.
The company captures surplus renewable energy as hydrogen for later electricity production to provide stable zero-emission power.
TechnipFMC provides offshore integration abilities in production systems and umbilicals, risers, and flowlines to store hydrogen subsea and transport it ashore.
Contributions to greenhouse gas removal include carbon transportation and storage (CTS) leveraging existing equipment and integration expertise.
TechnipFMC offers Dynamic Inter Array Cable Systems for transferring power from floating offshore wind farms to the onshore grid.
The company has experience delivering large-scale industrialized subsea projects and manufacturing capacity transferable from the oil and gas industry.
PC&S Company LLC is interested in partnering with DOE Labs for feedstocks for sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), marine, railway, and land transportation.
Feedstock options include lignin, bamboo tree, jatropha, camilina, and coconut oil.
The company notes an abundant supply of raw materials to produce lignin by volume for jet fuel production.
Bamboo trees can grow in the USA and be used to produce methanol and lignin.
There is a big market for methanol supply from shipping cargo companies in Europe that currently buy from China.
Shipping cargo engines are powered by M.A.N. shipping engines running on 100% methanol.
Polykala Technologies has worked on critical materials (including rare earth metals) recovery and production from scraps and post-consumer products for over 3 years.
The company uses a pilot plant scale hydrogen processing facility for this work.
It also processes critical minerals recovery with a custom-made plasma facility at a current scale of 50 kg/day.
Other business areas include catalyst and membrane manufacturing.
Hydroplane Ltd. develops and tests low temperature PEM fuel cell membrane electric assemblies.
The company develops and tests low temperature PEM fuel cell technology from component to stack level.
It develops and tests fuel cell balance of plant technology including thermal management, power distribution, and software control.
Hydroplane develops electric propulsion powered by fuel cell technology for aviation, maritime, and ground vehicles.
The company performs aircraft system integration and test with electric propulsion.
It conducts fuel cell stack system integration and test including up to 200 kW load bank and humidification systems.
Hydroplane designs and tests low temperature PEM thermal management.
The company designs, fabricates, and tests fuel cell gaskets.
It performs endurance testing of H2 fuel cell stacks.
Hydroplane designs liquid hydrogen tanks and fuel delivery systems.
Brookhaven National Laboratory's Interdisciplinary Science Department performs research to help meet the Nation's exponentially growing energy needs.

The Energy Systems Division conducts research for electric grid modernization, renewable energy system deployment, energy system efficiency and emissions, geothermal energy system materials, and advanced manufacturing methods.

Energy Conversion research offers advanced technical solutions to reduce fossil fuel use in residential and commercial building energy applications, focusing on biofuel end use, combustion efficiency and emissions, and system concepts.
Geothermal Materials research focuses on developing new materials for geothermal well applications to improve system performance.
Renewables and Grid Modernization work supports development of solutions for deployment, grid integration, and operation of renewable energy technologies in the US.
The Energy Storage Division conducts leading-edge research into promising energy storage technologies for consumer electronics, vehicle technologies, and grid-level storage.
The lab has dry room facilities to assemble pouch cells up to 2Ah.
It has expertise in mechanistic characterization of electrochemical energy storage processes from material to systems level.

Vehicle Technologies research includes study and preparation of novel materials and electrodes for high energy, high power, and safe batteries capable of fast charge and function in extreme environments.
Grid-level Storage research focuses on developing electrochemical energy storage systems based on sustainable materials for safe, long-life batteries.

Shorepower Technologies provides expertise in Electric Vehicle Service Equipment design, charging equipment options, installation at community and interstate site-host locations, battery-energy-storage for grid balancing, engineering, project management, procurement, networked connectivity, customer payment options, 24/7 public access operations, and utilization/environmental benefits reporting.
The company's existing Truck Stop Electrification sites already have infrastructure available to tie-in EV charging equipment.
Shorepower saves on project time and costs, allowing for more EV spaces and L2 and/or DC-Fast charging equipment in project budgets.
As a minority-owned business enterprise (MBE), Shorepower helps organizations meet diversity goals.
The company has support from the DOT-DOE Joint Office/ MBDA agency to advance public-private partnerships.
DRG is a full-service engineering and research firm focused on the electric utility and infrastructure sector.
Areas of expertise include transmission and distribution planning, grid automation, reliability, and resiliency.

Specific capabilities include Electric Power System research, Electric Distribution Engineering Design, Electrical Substation Design, Electric Grid Automation, advanced sensing and fault detection for Electric Power systems, situational awareness for Electric Power systems, HPC high-performance computing Electric Power supply, electrical protection and controls, AI and Machine Learning for Electric Power systems, renewable generation (pv, wind, hydro, nuclear, energy storage, EV charging stations), and Electrical Power Quality and Monitoring.
ORNL hosts the Battery Manufacturing Facility (BMF).
The BMF analyzes every aspect of battery cell development from raw materials and electrode dispersion preparation to finished product and performance testing.

The facility houses equipment for researching advanced materials, electrolyte development, electrode formulation chemistry, slurry rheology, innovative coating technology, and high-performance electrode architectures.
Resources include three coating lines, a 1,400 ft2 dry room, and a cell assembly line.
The BMF can produce pouch cells up to 66 × 99 × 12 mm and 6 Ah capacity.
The facility houses state-of-the-art characterization capabilities including XPS, XRD, SEM, GC-MS, and electrochemical performance testing with several Maccor and Biologic cyclers.
The University of Maine's Center for Research on Sustainable Forests and Forest Policy and Economics Lab researches integrated approaches to forest resource issues.
The lab conducts economic analysis of climate change mitigation and adaptation.
It has developed forest biomass and carbon models of varying spatial and temporal scales.
Models investigate estimating global timber supply and carbon stocks under different socioeconomic and policy conditions.
Research includes understanding how landowners prioritize forest management for ecosystem services like carbon and biomass.
The lab is interested in partnering with DOE labs to integrate remote sensing and economic modeling tools to improve quantifying forest carbon stocks and fluxes throughout the US.
It aims to advance forest economic models to identify, assess, and address economic drivers in forest management regarding carbon impact.
Models can estimate forest carbon, timber market, and ecosystem impacts at stand, regional, national, and international scales.
Recent projects evaluated the impact of forest carbon offsets on timber supply, biomass energy, and wood product markets.
The lab has conducted analyses on forest carbon market concerns about leakage, additionality, and permanence.
The Agile BioFoundry (ABF) is a consortium of seven U.S. DOE national laboratories operating as a distributed biofoundry with industry and academia.
It is funded by DOE’s Bioenergy Technologies Office (BETO).
ABF work supports BETO’s goals to develop sustainable aviation fuels and decarbonize energy-intensive industries.
The consortium has developed an extensive design-build-test-learn platform with scale-up, techno-economic and life-cycle analyses, and onboarding capabilities.
Goals include actively developing funds-in and BETO-funded projects for technology transfer to industry.
ABF aims to develop engineering biology, computational, and bioprocess tools to advance strain performance for sustainable fuel and chemical production.
It develops innovative pathways, strains, and processes for sustainable aviation fuels.
It develops innovative pathways, strains, and processes for direct replacement and performance-advantaged chemicals.
SLAC focuses on reducing carbon emissions in industrial processes like steelmaking, cement, and chemical production.
The lab develops, understands, and integrates materials and devices for electrocatalysis and battery technologies.
It constructs a toolbox of innovative techniques including synchrotron X-ray spectroscopy and imaging to understand sustainable manufacturing, electrocatalysis, and battery technologies.
NYCSBUS is an 800 bus school bus company in NYC operating out of six depots.
The organization is rapidly electrifying and demonstrating vehicle to grid (V2G) and vehicle to building (V2B) technology.
As a non-profit, it is organized to innovate for its industry and supports demonstrations of new technology, provides operator-level insights, and helps introduce new technologies.
EVenergi streamlines the transition to zero-emission vehicles with intelligent software solutions for planning and managing fleets.
The company developed and markets the BetterFleet platform in the United States, Europe, and Asia Pacific.
EVenergi's vision is to accelerate the transition to zero emission fleets efficiently from financial, operational, and environmental perspectives.
Founded in 2016, the company serves over 200 complex fleets across North America, Europe, Singapore, and Australia.

Using real-world data and road-tested algorithms, BetterFleet simplifies planning through automation in charger and vehicle selection, utility infrastructure sizing, and operational planning optimization.
The platform manages live charging and operations to meet financial and sustainability goals without disrupting core duties or impacting the electricity grid.
BetterFleet uses "digital twin" and machine learning technologies to simulate fleet operations accurately and optimize performance.
The UC Merced group focuses on electric and robotic vehicles.
It is interested in combining power conversion with advanced control and optimization methods to achieve high energy efficiency, durability, and reliability of energy storage systems.
Capabilities include controls and optimization, power conversion, energy management, thermal management, and hardware in the loop validation.
Framatome has decades of experience in fuel manufacturing and testing, recently celebrating its 50th anniversary at the Richland, WA plant.

Engineers have designed and built most fuel manufacturing processes from chemical conversion of UF6 to UO2, powder processing, ceramic pelletizing, fuel rod fabrication, and fuel assembly fabrication.
The company continues investment in its Richland site and maintains technology leadership in nuclear fuel fabrication.
Framatome's technology has been licensed to other fuel fabricators worldwide.
Manufacturing equipment satisfies requirements using rigorous integrated safety analyses, criticality safety, and accident scenarios.
Framatome is a fully integrated manufacturer of zirconium alloy products, mastering chemical and metallurgical processes at facilities in France.
It is the only zirconium producer with its own integrated R&D facility.
The Zr cladding manufacturing facility in Paimboeuf, France, is qualifying multiple full-length physical vapor deposition (PVD) coating machines to commercialize Cr coatings.
This coating technology can optimize coatings for SiC-based cladding designs.
The Richland facility fabricates fuel supporting the entire US LWR fleet, including 11x11 BWR and PWR fuel designs with lattices from 14x14 to 17x17, plus a new design for the SMR market.
Fuel fabrication equipment allows easy change out between designs to service the US LWR fleet.
Framatome is leading advanced R&D with new fuel-cladding materials like silicon carbide and advanced PVD coating technologies.

Hollingsworth & Vose Company (H&V) is a privately held, family-owned manufacturer of advanced nonwoven materials and engineered papers specializing in filtration media, battery separators, and energy storage solutions.
The company formalized as a partnership in 1881 between Zachery Hollingsworth and Charles Vose.
H&V operates 13 manufacturing facilities and nine research centers across the Americas, Europe, China, and India.
The company employs over 2,200 people worldwide, including more than 40 PhDs dedicated to innovation.

H&V's commitment to sustainability underscores its development of advanced cure resin binders in 2003 and activated carbon technologies, positioning it as a key supplier for electric vehicle energy storage and high-performance filtration amid growing demands for environmental efficiency.

These materials find applications in automotive sectors for electric vehicle battery components, HVAC systems for air purification in commercial buildings and homes, medical and biopharmaceutical environments for cleanroom and sterile filtration, and broader industrial uses in heavy-duty equipment and process protection.

The company's competitive advantages stem from custom engineering tailored to specific performance needs—such as high-tensile synthetic-glass blends for battery separators—rather than reliance on mass-produced commodities, supported by over 280 patents and a research team exceeding 40 PhDs dedicated to innovation in clean air, water, and energy technologies.

With 13 manufacturing facilities strategically distributed across three continents—North America, Europe, and Asia—H&V ensures proximity to key markets, enabling rapid adaptation to demands for robust components like those in high-efficiency filtration systems and lithium-based energy storage.

Facilities proliferated domestically and internationally, including sites in Floyd, Virginia (1976), Winchcombe, United Kingdom (1982), Apizaco, Mexico (1983 via joint venture in 1981), Hawkinsville, Georgia (1988), and Corvallis, Oregon (1997).

Sustained family ownership across generations facilitated consistent investments in research and development, allowing innovations such as high-efficiency synthetic filtration media (1991), activated carbon technology AFM™ (1998), and nanofiber coatings (1999).

In the early 2000s, Hollingsworth & Vose expanded its international footprint to better serve global customers and bolster supply chain resilience, opening a manufacturing facility in Suzhou, China, in 2004.

Additional regional operations in Mexico and India further supported localized production and distribution, enabling the company to mitigate risks from geopolitical disruptions and fluctuating regional demands.

Amid the COVID-19 pandemic, Hollingsworth & Vose demonstrated manufacturing agility by pivoting production in 2020 to supply critical filtration materials for N95 respirators, ventilators, medical gowns, hoods, and diagnostic test components.

As an essential business, the company ramped up productivity to meet surging global demand for personal protective equipment (PPE) filter media, while also providing battery separators for medical devices and emergency power systems in hospitals and transport.
In response to escalating global demands for sustainable technologies, Hollingsworth & Vose has emphasized advanced materials for electric vehicles (EVs) and clean air applications since the 2010s.

Its Absorbent Glass Mat (AGM) battery separators, featuring porous fiberglass designs, enhance EV performance by improving charge efficiency, preventing short circuits, and enabling spill-proof, maintenance-free systems suitable for renewable energy storage and grid stabilization.
Concurrently, the company's filtration media supports clean air intake in power generation and HVAC systems, aligning with broader trends toward reduced emissions and improved indoor air quality.

Hollingsworth & Vose produces advanced separator materials for lead-acid batteries, including absorbent glass mat (AGM) and gel variants, engineered to balance ionic conductivity with electrical insulation to prevent internal shorts.

These separators, such as EnergyGuard®, a synthetic-glass composite, offer high tensile and burst strength to resist punctures from pasting lumps or mechanical stress, while facilitating electrolyte absorption and gas recombination in valve-regulated lead-acid (VRLA) systems.

In AGM applications, EnergyGuard® demonstrates superior vibration resistance and deeper cycling capability compared to traditional materials, contributing to extended battery life in high-demand uses like industrial forklifts and uninterruptible power supplies.

Hi-Sep™ separators, composed of synthetic fibers, micro-glass fibers, binders, and inorganic fillers, target gel and flooded lead-acid batteries with tortuous pore structures that block dendritic growth—metallic filaments that can cause shorts—while maintaining low electrical resistance and porosity exceeding 70% for efficient ion transport.

This design supports partial state-of-charge (PSOC) and deep-cycle operations, with high thermal durability to withstand elevated temperatures without degradation, reducing failure risks in motive power and reserve power applications.
Empirical testing shows Hi-Sep™ exhibits the lowest electrochemical oxidation rates in its class, minimizing impurity-related degradation and enhancing overall battery stability.

For primary batteries, H&V's micro-glass fiber separators in lithium thionyl chloride cells prioritize electrolyte stability and low ionic resistance, ensuring consistent performance across wide temperature ranges without leaching impurities that could compromise safety.

These materials act as electron insulators while permitting lithium-ion diffusion, critical for long-shelf-life applications in industrial metering and sensors, where thermal runaway risks are mitigated by inherent low impurity levels and lightweight construction.
Hollingsworth & Vose maintains its corporate headquarters at 112 Washington Street in East Walpole, Massachusetts, which serves as the original operational site dating back to the company's founding.
The firm operates 13 facilities across North America, Europe, and Asia, enabling localized production of nonwoven materials for filtration, battery, and industrial uses.

In North America, key manufacturing facilities are located in West Groton, Massachusetts (219 Townsend Road); Greenwich and Easton, New York; Floyd, Virginia (289 Park View Road); Hawkinsville, Georgia (106 Industrial Boulevard); and Corvallis, Oregon (1115 SE Crystal Lake Drive).

These plants focus on producing specialized media, such as battery separators and air filtration materials, with historical expansions including a New York site in the 1950s and a Virginia facility in the 1970s.
European operations include sites in Hatzfeld, Germany (Berleburger Strasse 71); Winchcombe, England (Postlip Mills); and Kentmere, England (Waterford Bridge).
Facilities in Germany were constructed progressively during the 2000s to enhance production capabilities for European markets.

In Asia, the company established a facility in Suzhou, China (No. 39 Song Bei Road, Suzhou Industrial Park), opened in 2004, which houses the Mark Hollingsworth Global Test Center for advanced testing and development of filtration and battery technologies.
Latin American manufacturing is supported by a plant in Apizaco, Tlaxcala, Mexico, stemming from a joint venture formed in 1981.

The network of facilities collectively underpins global supply chains, with integrated R&D at select sites like Suzhou enabling rapid prototyping and scale-up of nonwoven innovations, though specific output capacities remain proprietary and undisclosed in public records.
Hollingsworth & Vose maintains a vertically integrated supply chain for key raw materials such as cellulose fibers and resins, which are essential for its advanced nonwoven materials production.

This integration spans from fiber sourcing to final product manufacturing, enabling control over quality, cost efficiencies, and lead times while reducing vulnerabilities to global supply disruptions, such as those experienced in recent years from geopolitical tensions and raw material shortages.

Approximately 50% of the company's products incorporate cellulose, sourced through suppliers adhering to Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) chain-of-custody standards to ensure responsible forestry practices.

The company's global network of 13 manufacturing facilities across North America, Europe, Asia, and Latin America supports logistics efficiency by localizing production near key markets and customers, thereby minimizing transportation costs and enhancing responsiveness to demand fluctuations.

Suppliers are vetted via a Supplier Code of Conduct emphasizing integrity, environmental protection, and human rights, with sustainability assessments integrated into requests for quotes and contracts through partnerships like EcoVadis.
This structure has allowed Hollingsworth & Vose to sustain operations amid broader industry challenges, passing cost savings to customers without compromising material performance.

As a family-owned enterprise for seven generations, Hollingsworth & Vose employs more than 2,200 workers worldwide, with a distribution of 47% in the United States, 31% in Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and India, 15% in Asia-Pacific, and 5% in Mexico.

The model promotes long-term retention through targeted development programs, including a Global Associates rotational initiative for early-career talent, over 35 custom certification courses via LinkedIn Learning, and leadership training like the Management Development Program, which graduated 170 participants in its inaugural year.
Emphasis is placed on skilled roles, evidenced by a research team comprising over 40 PhDs, aligning with the demands of high-value manufacturing in filtration and energy storage.

Labor relations include coverage of some employees under collective bargaining agreements, with policies prohibiting discrimination, harassment, and unethical practices; no incidents of discrimination, child labor, or forced labor were reported in 2024.
While isolated employee feedback notes tensions between union representatives and management, no major strikes or significant union controversies appear in public records.

Retention efforts are bolstered by comprehensive benefits, including parental leave, mental health resources, and recognition platforms like HiFive for milestone and performance awards, alongside safety metrics targeting a Total Recordable Incident Rate below 1.1.

Hollingsworth & Vose developed wet-laid nonwoven technologies to address the need for uniform fiber dispersion in filtration media, enabling precise control over pore structure and filtration efficiency in industrial applications.

This process, which adapts papermaking techniques to synthetic and glass fibers, allows for the creation of composite media with enhanced mechanical strength and dirt-holding capacity compared to traditional dry-laid methods.

In glass fiber composites, the company integrated micro-glass fibers with binders to form durable structures that improve airflow resistance and particle capture, driven by requirements for high-efficiency filtration in harsh environments like power generation and transportation.
These advancements resulted from iterative material formulations tested for empirical performance metrics, such as pressure drop and efficiency ratings under standardized ASTM protocols.

For battery separators, Hollingsworth & Vose secured patents on designs featuring controlled pore structures and low apparent density, which optimize electrolyte wettability and ionic conductivity to support higher energy density in lead-acid and advanced cells.

For instance, separators with specified basis weight, surface area, and density parameters enable reduced internal resistance, as verified through electrochemical testing for recombination efficiency and cycle life in valve-regulated lead-acid (VRLA) batteries.

The patented EnergyGlass™ technology employs all-glass absorbed glass mat (AGM) constructions, providing superior compression resistance and acid management derived from the practical demands of automotive starting-lighting-ignition systems.

These innovations evolved from early military-grade battery needs for reliability under vibration, progressing through empirical refinements to accommodate lithium-ion separators for electric vehicle applications, where enhanced thermal stability and shutdown properties mitigate dendrite formation risks.

Hollingsworth & Vose developed filter media for the U.S. military in 1941, which served as the foundation for the company's HEPA air filtration media, enabling scalable production of media that captured submicron particles critical for wartime applications.

This innovation, refined post-World War II, supported the expansion of HEPA systems into cleanroom environments by providing uniform, high-efficiency microfiber glass media that met stringent particulate control requirements, influencing standards in semiconductor manufacturing and pharmaceutical production where contamination levels below 0.3 microns are essential.

In defense sectors, the company's early military-grade media contributed to reliable air filtration systems, enhancing operational resilience in contaminated environments and bolstering U.S. technological advantages through durable, high-flow materials tested under extreme conditions.

For automotive applications, Hollingsworth & Vose supplied advanced battery separators that extended service life and improved reliability in start-stop systems and hybrid vehicles, with materials designed to withstand electrochemical stresses and reduce internal short-circuit risks, supporting the sector's shift toward efficient energy storage.

The NanoWave media received the 2013 IDEA Achievement Award from the International Nonwovens Technical Conference for its multi-layer design, which delivered 2.4 times the surface area of standard flat-sheet media, more than double the dust-holding capacity, and lower airflow resistance, thereby enhancing filtration durability and efficiency in HVAC, gas turbine, and liquid applications while enabling energy recovery through incineration.

Similarly, the 2000 introduction of Technostat electret media represented the highest-performing charged filtration technology of its era, achieving superior particle capture rates that reduced maintenance intervals in industrial settings.
These advancements provided quantifiable improvements in system longevity and performance metrics, such as extended media lifespan under high-dust loads.

In August 2024, Hollingsworth & Vose launched PlusZero™, a line of high-performance filtration media and battery separators manufactured without per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), responding to increasing regulatory scrutiny of these persistent chemicals.

The products utilize alternative synthetic materials that deliver comparable filtration efficiency and durability to PFAS-containing predecessors, enabling applications in air and liquid filtration as well as energy storage systems without performance trade-offs.
This development followed several years of internal research aimed at phasing out PFAS while preserving product efficacy, positioning the company ahead of broader industry mandates.

The company has invested in energy-efficient manufacturing processes and waste minimization, as outlined in its 2024 Sustainability Report, which sets targets including a 25% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 greenhouse gas emissions by 2030 relative to 2022 baselines.

entity_name: Top 19 Automotive Parts Manufacturing Companies in Georgia
facts: Georgia's automotive parts manufacturing industry is an intricate system of companies specializing in producing vital components for vehicles.
The sector features enterprises ranging from family-owned businesses to large corporations crafting precision turned parts to environmentally friendly plastic components.
With rapid growth of electric and hybrid vehicles, manufacturers are pivoting towards sustainable practices and advanced technologies.
Many firms are responding to heightened demand for innovative automotive solutions.
The list includes 19 automotive parts manufacturing companies based in Georgia.
Firms range from small private operations to larger entities employing hundreds to thousands of workers.
Companies were founded between the 1960s and 2023.
Specializations include stamped parts, suspension components, and recycled automotive parts.
Locations span from Lavonia to Atlanta.
Haering Precision USA LP is a subsidiary of Anton Häring KG and a family-owned precision parts manufacturer based in Lavonia, Georgia.
Haering Precision USA LP was founded in 1961 and employs around 288 workers.
The company specializes in high-quality precision turned parts for traditional combustion engines, hybrid and electric vehicles, and hydrogen fuel cells.
Haering Precision USA LP received the Global Supplier Award from Bosch and the Premium Supplier Award from Schaeffler.
Jefferson Southern Corporation is based in Rockmart, Georgia, established in 2001 with a headcount of 201-500.
The company focuses on high-quality stamped and welded components for vehicle structural integrity and performance.
Jefferson Southern Corporation operates as a Tier One supplier providing parts ready for installation to the vehicle's interior shell or 'white body'.
Ajin Georgia is based in Register, Georgia, founded in 2023 with a headcount of 501-1000.
The company specializes in OEM parts for vehicle production and supports Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Ajin Georgia made a recent $40,000 donation to local organizations.
F&P Georgia is based in Rome, Georgia, established in 2000 with a headcount of 201-500.
The company specializes in suspension components including subframes, lower arms, and pedals.
F&P Georgia operates as a tier one supplier under the Ftech Inc. network.
Sewon America Inc. is based in Rincon, Georgia, established in 1985 with over 1000 employees.
The company specializes in automotive stamping and interior parts while investing in R&D for manufacturing processes.
Trenton Pressing is based in Trenton, Georgia, established in 1995 with a headcount of 201-500.
The company operates from over 192,000 square feet across three facilities and serves as a direct Tier One supplier to BMW and Club Car.
KTX America Corporation is based in Toccoa, Georgia, also known as NIFCO KTW America, established in 2016 with a headcount of 201-500.
The company received $47.6M in funding in April 2014 and specializes in plastic components for vehicle interiors and exteriors.
KTX America Corporation serves BMW and Daimler using multi-component injection molding and high-gloss painting.
Hyundai MOBIS Alabama, LLC - Georgia Plant is based in West Point, Georgia, established in 2007 with a headcount of 501-1000.
The facility assembles and supplies chassis modules, cockpit modules, and front-end modules to support KIA's manufacturing operations via just-in-sequence delivery.
Ecoplastic America Corporation is based in Register, Georgia, founded in 1966 with a headcount of 501-1000.
The company operates as a tier-1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group producing bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.
Ecoplastic America Corporation is actively building a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County, Georgia, expected to create numerous jobs.
Honda Lock of America is based in Bremen, Georgia, also known as Minebea Access Solutions, founded in 1962 with approximately 207 employees.
The company specializes in automotive and machinery safety and security components for four-wheeled and two-wheeled vehicles.
Novelis is an aluminum manufacturer based in Atlanta, Georgia, founded in 2005 with over 10,000 employees.
Novelis is the largest recycler of aluminum worldwide and supplies aluminum sheet and lightweight battery enclosures for electric vehicles.
The company received $750.0M in debt funding in January 2025 and is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited.
Piolax is based in Canton, Georgia, founded in 1931 with around 396 employees.
The company specializes in plastic and metal fastening products including trim clips, fuel valves, and glove box handles.
Piolax received a $432,000 grant in March 2024.
FuelTech USA is based in Ball Ground, Georgia, founded in 2003 with around 21 employees.
The company specializes in automotive performance products including engine management systems, wiring solutions, and ignition systems for the motorsports industry.
Boles Parts Supply Inc is based in Atlanta, Georgia, founded in 1964 with a headcount of 11-50.
The company specializes in remanufactured components including electrical parts, A/C products, and brake components while offering reverse logistics services.
Boles Parts Supply Inc operates over 1,000,000 square feet of warehouse space.
Road Tested Parts is a family-owned company based in Carnesville, Georgia, founded in 2021 with a headcount of 201-500.
The company distributes recycled OEM automotive parts to over 50,000 repair shops throughout the Southeastern United States.
Road Tested Parts expanded through strategic acquisitions of established automotive recycling businesses.
ALBAform is based in Flowery Branch, Georgia, founded in 2011 with a headcount of 51-200.

The company specializes in custom metal fabrication for automotive and e-mobility sectors, producing interior parts for automotive seating such as seat cushion structures and dashboard reinforcements.
ALBAform operates facilities in the USA, Mexico, and Czech Republic and is a 100% women-owned business.
Auto Metal Direct is based in Gainesville, Georgia, founded in 2006 with a headcount of 51-200.
The company specializes in restoration parts for classic American cars and trucks including body panels, trim, and glass, and participates in the General Motors Restoration Parts Program.
Lawson Industries, Inc. is based in Albany, Georgia, founded in 1998 with 23 employees.
The company specializes in automotive exhaust systems and components including muffler clamps, performance exhausts, and catalytic converters while serving customers across 39 states.

canonical_name: Alvin W. Vogtle Electric Generating Plant
facts: The official name of the facility is the Alvin W. Vogtle Electric Generating Plant.
The plant is located in Burke County, Georgia, near Waynesboro.
The facility status is operational.
Construction for Units 1–2 began on August 1, 1976.
Construction for Unit 3 began on March 12, 2013.
Construction for Unit 4 began on November 19, 2013.
Unit 1 was commissioned on June 1, 1987.
Unit 2 was commissioned on May 20, 1989.
Unit 3 was commissioned on July 31, 2023.
Unit 4 was commissioned on April 29, 2024.
Construction costs for Units 1–2 were $8.87 billion in 1989 USD, equivalent to $19.5 billion in 2024 dollars.
Construction costs for Units 3–4 are $36.8 billion.
Georgia Power owns a 45.7% interest in the plant.
Oglethorpe Power Corporation owns a 30% interest in the plant.
MEAG owns a 22.7% interest in the plant.
Dalton Utilities owns a 1.6% interest in the plant.
Southern Nuclear operates the facility.
The reactor type is a pressurized water reactor (PWR).
Westinghouse is the reactor supplier.
The plant utilizes four natural draft cooling towers.
The Savannah River serves as the plant's cooling source.
Thermal capacity for Units 1–2 is 2 × 3626 MWth.
Thermal capacity for Units 3–4 is 2 × 3400 MWth.
Unit 1 has a power generation capacity of 1 × 1150 MWe.
Unit 2 has a power generation capacity of 1 × 1152 MWe.
Units 3 and 4 each have a power generation capacity of 1 × 1117 MWe.
The plant's total nameplate capacity is 4536 MWe.
The capacity factor was 95.09% in 2017 and 91.25% over its lifetime.
The annual net output reached 33,871 GWh in 2024.
With Units 3 & 4 operational as of 2025, it is the largest nuclear power plant in the United States.
It is the only nuclear plant in the country with four reactors.
The plant is named after former Alabama Power and Southern Company board chairman Alvin Vogtle.
Units 1 and 2 utilize Westinghouse 4-loop PWRs paired with General Electric steam turbines and electric generators.
Each of Units 1 & 2 has a gross electricity generation capacity of 1,215 MW, for a combined capacity of 2,430 MW.
The twin natural-draft cooling towers are 548 ft (167 m) tall.
Four smaller mechanical draft cooling towers provide nuclear service cooling water to safety and auxiliary components.
The Nuclear Regulatory Commission renewed licenses for Units 1 & 2 in 2009 for an additional 20 years, extending to January 16, 2047 for Unit 1 and September 2, 2049 for Unit 2.
Capital investment for Units 1–2 jumped from an estimated $660 million to $8.87 billion during construction.
Preliminary construction for two additional units utilizing Westinghouse AP1000 reactors began in 2009.
The two new cooling towers for Units 3 & 4 are nearly 600 ft (180 m) tall.
Certified construction and capital costs for Units 3–4 were originally $14 billion in a 2017 report, later increasing to $34 billion by 2023.
Westinghouse Electric Company filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2017 due to losses from nuclear construction projects.
Southern Nuclear took over construction from Westinghouse on July 31, 2017.
Bechtel was selected as the day-to-day construction manager on August 31, 2017.
President Barack Obama announced $8.33 billion in federal loan guarantees toward construction costs on February 16, 2010.
Georgia Power's share of the expected building cost for the two new reactors was around $6.1 billion.
The Department of Energy approved a $6.5 billion loan guarantee for Georgia Power and Oglethorpe Power Corp. in February 2014.
Further federal loan guarantees of $3.7 billion were provided in March 2019, bringing total federal loan guarantees to $12 billion.
The Georgia Public Service Commission approved continuation of construction on December 21, 2017, with conditions reducing recoverable costs from ratepayers.
A scheduled monthly consumer rate increase of $3.78 was expected after first power following the GPSC approval.
The February 2018 Vogtle Construction Monitoring Report approved November 2021 and November 2022 as target in-service dates for Units 3 & 4.
A $2.3 billion increase in costs was recognized in August 2018, bringing the total estimated cost to about $25 billion including financing costs.
In September 2018, Georgia Power agreed to pay an additional proportion of completion costs for smaller project partners if costs exceeded $9.2 billion.
Unit 3 began commercial operations on July 31, 2023, marking the first new nuclear reactor in the United States in 7 years.
As of FY 2024 Q3, Georgia Power's net capital costs incurred for Units 3-4 were $10.65 billion total, with an additional estimated $83 million in completion costs.
This cost figure is inclusive of $1.2 billion not shared with other owners, net of ~$1.9 billion received from Toshiba in settlement and customer refunds.
Georgia Power's 45.7% ownership implies a total capitalized construction cost of $23.76 billion for Units 3-4.
Non-capitalized financing charges (interest) totaled $3.53 billion by Georgia Power, recovered via ratepayer surcharges before completion.
In 2008, reactors 1 and 2 were increased in power by 1.7% via an Appendix K uprate.
The NRC approved Vogtle's License Amendment Request in March 2008 to increase reactor power output through more accurate feedwater flow measurement.
Unit 1 was uprated during its Spring 2008 refueling outage, and Unit 2 during the Fall 2008 outage.
A loss of electrical power incident occurred on March 20, 1990, when a truck backed into a support column in the 230kV switchyard.
Plant operators declared a site area emergency at 9:40 a.m., downgraded to an alert at 10:15 a.m., and terminated it at 1:47 p.m. after power restoration.
The electrical fault also affected Unit 2, causing breakers to trip and leading to a full load rejection, turbine trip, and reactor scram.
Southern Nuclear formally applied for an Early Site Permit for two additional units in August 2006.
An application for a Combined Construction and Operating License was submitted in March 2008.
Georgia Power Company reached a contract agreement for two AP1000 reactors designed by Westinghouse, owned by Toshiba, in April 2008.
Westinghouse partnered with the Shaw Group and its Stone & Webster division to manage the project.
The contract received approval from the Georgia Public Service Commission in March 2009.
The NRC issued an Early Site Permit and Limited Work Authorization in August 2009.
A 19th revision for AP1000 Design Certification was written in December 2011 following the Fukushima accident, requiring a complete redesign of the containment building.
The NRC approved the construction license for the two proposed AP1000 reactors in February 2012.
NRC Chairman Gregory Jaczko cast the lone dissenting vote citing safety concerns from Fukushima.

Environmental and anti-nuclear groups sued to stop the expansion project one week after the license was received, but the lawsuit was rejected by the Washington D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals on July 11, 2012.
Construction contractor Shaw was purchased by Chicago Bridge & Iron Company (CB&I) in February 2013.
CB&I exited the project due to delays and cost overruns in early 2015, leading Westinghouse to take direct control and hire Fluor.
Southern Company and Westinghouse added Bechtel to the project in 2016 to share construction management responsibilities.
A three-month delay to completion was announced in October 2021, pushing Unit 3 to Q3 2022 and Unit 4 to Q2 2023.
In August 2022, a further delay pushed Unit 3 to Q1 2023 and Unit 4 to Q4 2023, with costs expected to rise over $30 billion.
Vogtle Unit 3 began loading nuclear fuel on October 14, 2022.
During start-up testing in February 2023, the plant's cooling system suffered unexpected vibrations, delaying regular service to May or June 2023.
Vogtle Unit 3 reached criticality for the first time on March 6, 2023, connected to the grid on April 1, and entered commercial operation on July 31.
Georgia Power announced Unit 4 completed hot functional testing on May 2, 2023.
Fuel loading began for Unit 4 on August 18, 2023, with 264 fuel elements.
A reactor coolant pump motor fault in Unit 4 in October 2023 delayed its in-service date to Q1 2024.
Cooling system vibrations found later pushed the in-service date to Q2 2024.
Vogtle Unit 4 reached criticality on February 14, 2024, connected to the grid on March 1, and entered commercial operation on April 29.
The NRC defines two emergency planning zones: a 10-mile plume exposure pathway zone and a 50-mile ingestion pathway zone.
In 2010, the population within 10 miles was 5,845, and within 50 miles was 726,640.
Cities within 50 miles include Augusta, located 26 miles from the city center.
The NRC estimated the annual risk of an earthquake causing core damage at 1 in 140,845 per year based on an August 2010 study.
In late 2024, global demand for lithium-ion batteries passed 1 terawatt-hour per year.
As of late 2024, global production capacity for lithium-ion batteries was more than twice the demand.
In 2010, global lithium-ion battery production capacity was 20 gigawatt-hours.
By 2016, global production capacity reached 28 GWh, with 16.4 GWh in China.
Global production capacity was 767 GWh in 2020, with China accounting for 75%.
Predictions for lithium-ion battery production in 2023 range from 400 to 1,100 GWh.
NMC (lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide) and its derivatives are widely used in the electrification of transport.
Lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA) is another high-energy lithium-ion chemistry commonly used in electric vehicle batteries.
Three basic battery types are used in 2020s-era electric vehicles: cylindrical cells (e.g., Tesla), prismatic pouch (e.g., from LG), and prismatic can cells (e.g., from LG, Samsung, Panasonic).
Overall, between 1991 and 2024, prices for all types of lithium-ion cells fell approximately 99%.
From 1991 to 2005, the energy capacity per price of lithium-ion batteries improved more than ten-fold.
Silicon anodes enhanced with graphene nanotubes allow for record-breaking battery energy density of up to 350 Wh/kg and lower EV prices to be competitive with ICEs.
Standard lithium-ion batteries have a specific energy of 160–300 W·h/kg, with specialty versions reaching up to 450 W·h/kg.
Standard energy density for lithium-ion batteries is 250–842 W·h/L, while specialty versions reach up to 1,100 W·h/L.
The charge/discharge efficiency of lithium-ion batteries ranges from 80% to 90%.
Cycle durability for lithium-ion batteries typically ranges from 400 to 1,200 cycles.
The electrolyte salt in commercial lithium-ion cells is almost always lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6).
Copper is used as the current collector at the negative electrode, while aluminum is used for the positive electrode.
Lithium-ion batteries have enabled portable consumer electronics, laptop computers, cellular phones, and electric cars.
In the three decades since lithium-ion batteries were first sold in 1991, their volumetric energy density increased threefold while their cost dropped tenfold.

entity_name: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America
canonical_name: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America (YMMC)
facts: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America (YMMC) is a state-of-the-art U.S.-based manufacturing facility located in Newnan, Georgia.
The YMMC campus designs and manufactures four recreational product lines for worldwide distribution: All-Terrain Vehicles (ATV), Side-by-Sides (SxS), Personal Watercraft, and Golf Cars.
Since its founding in 1986, Yamaha has produced nearly 4 million vehicles at the YMMC facility.
The facility creates thousands of jobs across facilities in 11 U.S. states, including nearly 2,000 employees at the YMMC facility alone.
At YMMC, all Side-by-Side vehicles, full-size ATVs, and WaveRunners are manufactured for global distribution.
Production capacity at the facility is highlighted by an employee stating that every sixty seconds an ATV or a Golf Car rolls off the line.

The YMMC factory holds the highest certifications for quality, safety, and environmental manufacturing, specifically ISO9001 Quality Management System and ISO14001 Environmental Management System certifications.

Bob Brown, Vice President of Finance and Operations Support at Yamaha, stated that the company wanted to highlight vehicles built with pride on the line and the dedication and craftsmanship of the workforce.

Steve Nessl, Yamaha's Motorsports marketing manager, noted that everyone at Yamaha takes great pride in the U.S. production of their products and values the recognition of the Georgia factory workers' expertise and enthusiasm.
The facility was featured on the HISTORY Channel's season premiere of 'Modern Marvels: Machines,' which aired on Sunday, July 25th, with encore viewings available on History.com.

Yamaha Motor Corporation, USA (YMUS) maintains a corporate office in California, two corporate offices in Georgia, facilities in Wisconsin and Alabama, and factory operations in Tennessee and Georgia.

YMUS additional U.S.-based subsidiaries include Yamaha Marine Systems Company (with Bennett Marine in Florida and Kracor Systems in Wisconsin), Skeeter Boats (with division G3 Boats in Missouri), and Yamaha Precision Propeller (in Indiana).
ATV models over 90cc are recommended for use only by riders 16 years and older.
SxS vehicles are recommended for use only by licensed drivers 16 years and older.
source_title: Yamaha Motor USA Headlines Season Premiere of “Modern Marvels: Machines” on The HISTORY® Channel

facts: Alpharetta invested in optical fiber since the 1980s and hosts multiple data centers.
In 2019, the fintech industry in the Atlanta area was a driver of data center expansion in Alpharetta.
Data center expansion in Alpharetta is supported by tax breaks from Fulton County authorities.
Data center expansion ran into limitations from state regulations and local zoning authorities in 2024.
Capacity for data centers in Atlanta tripled from 200 MW to over 700 MW in 2023.
The Georgia Senate passed a bill to suspend data center tax exemptions on March 18, 2024.
On September 4, 2024, the Atlanta City Council passed rules limiting data center locations, prohibiting construction near the Beltline or within a half mile of MARTA rail stations.
From the first to the second half of 2023, data center projects in the area skyrocketed from over 235 MW to over 730 MW, equivalent to the output of a typical natural gas plant.
Tech North Atlanta (formerly known as Tech Alpharetta and the Alpharetta Technology Commission) is an advisory organization established by the City of Alpharetta in 2012.
Tech North Atlanta is an independent, 501(c)(6) nonprofit organization that aims to help Alpharetta lead in technology innovation.
The organization runs an advisory board of technology companies based in the city and holds monthly technology events for technology executives.
Tech North Atlanta operates the Tech Alpharetta Innovation Center, a technology startup incubator.
As of early 2020, about ten companies had graduated from the Tech Alpharetta incubator and were hiring employees in the North Fulton County region.
UPS Supply Chain Solutions is one of Alpharetta's top private sector employers, with 1,200 employees as of February 2026.
Alpharetta is not directly served by MARTA trains but is connected by multiple bus routes, and connecting the city to the rest of Metro Atlanta via heavy rail has been studied.
The company reports from Duluth, Ga., on November 06, 2025.

Fox Factory Holding Corp. is a global leader in the design, engineering, and manufacturing of premium products that deliver championship-level performance for specialty sports and on- and off-road vehicles.
Its portfolio of brands includes FOX, Marucci, Method Race Wheels, and others.
Fox Factory is a direct supplier of shocks, suspension, and components to leading powered vehicle and bicycle original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
The company offers premium baseball and softball gear and equipment through its global network of retailers, distributors, and direct-to-consumer channels.
Third Quarter Fiscal 2025 net sales increased 4.8% year-over-year to $376.4 million.
Year-to-date net sales for the first nine months of fiscal 2025 were up 6.3%.
Aftermarket Applications Group (AAG) net sales increased 17.4% to $117.8 million and rose 3.2% sequentially.
Powered Vehicles Group (PVG) net sales increased 15.1% to $125.9 million and rose 1.9% sequentially.
Gross margin improved 50 basis points year-over-year to 30.4%.
The company reported a net loss of $0.6 million, or $0.02 per diluted share, compared to net income of $4.8 million in the prior year quarter.
Adjusted EBITDA was $44.4 million, up 5.7% year-over-year, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 11.8%.
Adjusted earnings per diluted share were $0.23, down from $0.35 in the prior year quarter, reflecting higher tariff costs and strategic launch investments.
The company extended its credit agreement maturity through October 2030 to enhance financial flexibility.

CEO Mike Dennison stated that PVG segment growth was driven by strategic customer program launches and market share gains in motorcycle and powersports applications, which more than offset lower industry demand in automotive original equipment (OE) product lines.
The company is implementing a $25 million cost reduction program on track for full delivery this year and is preparing for phase two of this initiative.
Fox Factory addressed tariff headwinds during the quarter and made necessary investments in innovation and products, creating near-term margin pressure that was not in initial expectations.

Goodwill decreased from $639.5 million to $378.9 million due to a quantitative impairment assessment triggered by adverse changes to U.S. tariff policies, new and expanded tariffs enacted by the current presidential administration, and a sustained decline in the company's stock price.
Inventory increased by $7.4 million to $412.1 million, driven by planned inventory builds to support anticipated demand, foreign currency translation, and the impact from higher tariffs.
Total debt was $687.7 million as of October 3, 2025, down from $705.1 million as of January 3, 2025.
Cash and cash equivalents were $65.4 million as of October 3, 2025.
For the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, net sales are expected to be in the range of $340 million to $370 million.
For fiscal year 2025, net sales are expected to be in the range of $1.445 billion to $1.475 billion.

Management expects to begin phase two of its optimization initiative in fiscal 2026, focusing on operational initiatives to manage costs tightly toward margin improvement and free cash flow generation.
The company acquires complementary businesses to integrate engineering and manufacturing expertise beyond its core shock and suspension segment.
