facts: Novelis Inc. is a leading sustainable aluminum solutions provider and the world leader in aluminum rolling and recycling.
Novelis reported fourth quarter and full fiscal year 2026 financial results on May 19, 2026.
Full fiscal year 2026 net sales increased 7% versus the prior year to $18.4 billion.
Fiscal 2026 net income attributable to common shareholders decreased 98% versus the prior year to $15 million.
Full fiscal year 2026 Adjusted EBITDA was $1.6 billion, down 9% YoY.
Rolled product shipments for full FY2026 totaled 3,557 kilotonnes, down 5% YoY.
Novelis began commissioning the cold mill at its new U.S. greenfield plant in Bay Minette in March.
Total capital expenditures reached $2.3 billion in fiscal year 2026, mainly related to the Company's U.S. greenfield rolling and recycling plant in Bay Minette, Alabama.
The Bay Minette facility is expected to commission in the second half of calendar year 2026.
Novelis is focused on capturing strong market demand for high-recycled-content, low carbon aluminum.
Novelis serves customers in the aerospace, automotive, beverage packaging and specialties industries throughout North America, Europe, Asia and South America.
Steve Fisher serves as president and CEO of Novelis Inc.
Dev Ahuja serves as executive vice president and CFO of Novelis Inc.
Novelis expects to return to positive free cash flow by the end of fiscal 2027.
Novelis had a net leverage ratio of 4.1x at the end of fiscal year 2026.
Total liquidity stood at $2.8 billion as of March 31, 2026, consisting of $1.3 billion in cash and cash equivalents and $1.5 billion in availability under committed credit facilities.
Novelis is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, the metals flagship company of the Aditya Birla Group.
Oswego production interruptions caused rolled product shipments to be an estimated 145 kilotonnes lower than expected for full FY2026.
Novelis recorded $925 million in pre-tax losses related to fires at its Oswego, New York plant, net of insurance recoveries, for full FY2026.
Adjusted EBITDA for full FY2026 was impacted by an estimated negative $143 million from tariffs.
source_title: Novelis Reports Fourth Quarter and Full Fiscal Year 2026 Results | Morningstar

entity_name: Top 24 Electronic Manufacturing Companies in Georgia
canonical_name: Top 24 Electronic Manufacturing Companies in Georgia
facts: The electronic manufacturing industry in Georgia is a vital sector characterized by companies specializing in circuit board assembly to semiconductor fabrication, meeting demands for automotive electronics among other markets.
The sector is anticipated to grow driven by increasing consumer electronics consumption and technological integration across industries.
Investments in research and development enhance capabilities, making Georgia a hub for electronic manufacturing endeavors.
The list features 24 standout electronic manufacturing companies based in Georgia, established between 1983 and 2021.
These companies are primarily located in urban centers like Norcross, Alpharetta, and Atlanta.
They specialize in areas including PCB assembly, design engineering, and semiconductor packaging, catering to industries such as defense and telecommunications.
Engent, Inc. is headquartered in Norcross, Georgia, founded in 2002, with a headcount of 1001-5000 employees.
Engent specializes in high volume manufacturing, electronic assembly, prototyping, and engineering consulting.
Engent recently acquired rights to Siemens Dematic's advanced assembly technology to enhance manufacturing capabilities.
Prime Technological Services is headquartered in Suwanee, Georgia, founded in 1989, with a headcount of 201-500 employees.
Prime Technological Services specializes in PCB assembly and system integration, serving aerospace, medical, and telecommunications sectors.
Prime Technological Services operates four precision surface mount technology (SMT) lines and holds ISO certifications.
Printed Circuits Corp. is headquartered in Lilburn, Georgia, founded in 2000, with a headcount of 11-50 employees.
Printed Circuits Corp. operates from a 32,000 square foot facility and holds AS9100D and ISO 9001:2015 certifications.
Printed Circuits Corp. offers rapid prototyping, box builds, cable/wire harness assemblies, and reverse engineering services.
i-Tech e-Services is headquartered in Norcross, Georgia, founded in 1997, with a headcount of 11-50 employees.
i-Tech e-Services specializes in high-mix, low to mid-volume PCB assemblies supported by SMT lines equipped with MYDATA and Juki pick-and-place machines.
i-Tech e-Services holds AS9100D and ISO 13485 certifications and counts Panasonic USA and Georgia Power among its clients.
i-Tech e-Services received an award for being a valuable provider by Partnership Gwinnett in 2014.
Tier One, Inc. is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, founded in 2001, with a headcount of 1-10 employees.
Tier One, Inc. has designed over 1,000 complex printed circuit board assemblies and offers the Tier One Gateway IoT platform and GNSS modules.
PPI is headquartered in Athens, Georgia, founded in 1996, with a headcount of 51-200 employees.
PPI specializes in metal stamping, electronics manufacturing, and sustainable product development, and acquired South Dakota Partners for PCBA capabilities.
East West Manufacturing is headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia, founded in 2001, with a headcount of 1001-5000 employees.
East West Manufacturing secured approximately $76.9 million in funding in January 2022 and operates across the United States, Costa Rica, Canada, Mexico, and Vietnam.
Dujud is headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia, founded in 2019, with a headcount of 11-50 employees.
Dujud specializes in semiconductor research and additive manufacturing technologies, developing proprietary tech for the 3D printing of micro-electronics like circuit boards and antennas.
Gradient Technologies is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, founded in 2010, with a headcount of 11-50 employees.
Gradient Technologies serves consumer electronics, automotive, healthcare, and industrial automation sectors with teams in the United States, India, and Belgium.
Central One is headquartered in Fayetteville, Georgia, founded in 2010, with a headcount of 11-50 employees.
Central One holds ISO 9001:2015, ISO 14001, ISO 13485, and TS 16949 certifications and serves automotive, aerospace, medical, and consumer electronics sectors.
Silicon Creations is headquartered in Lawrenceville, Georgia, founded in 2006, with a headcount of 51-200 employees (approx. 150).
Silicon Creations specializes in silicon intellectual property solutions including phase-locked loops and serializer-deserializer interfaces, with IP in production across 5nm to 180nm process nodes.
ProBrand International, Inc. is headquartered in Marietta, Georgia, founded in 1983, with a headcount of 501-1000 employees.
ProBrand International invested in a state-of-the-art LNB manufacturing facility and provides low-power IoT solutions for agriculture, healthcare, and logistics.
Flip Electronics is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, founded in 2015, with a headcount of 51-200 employees (approx. 79).
Flip Electronics partnered with Ampleon to supply legacy RF transistors and serves military aerospace, medical, and industrial OEMs.
Innovative Circuits, Inc. is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, founded in 1998, with a headcount of 51-200 employees.
Innovative Circuits, Inc. holds ISO 9001:2015 certification and is ITAR registered, specializing in high-tech multilayer rigid PCBs and flexible circuits.
ArgonFDS is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, founded in 1990, with a headcount of 11-50 employees (approx. 103).
ArgonFDS manufactures rugged displays and computing devices for military applications and is part of the Spectra Aerospace & Defense group.
Delta Sigma Company is headquartered in Kennesaw, Georgia, founded in 1990, with a headcount of 11-50 employees.
Delta Sigma Company serves aerospace and automotive sectors, supported stealth F-117 Nighthawk and F-22 Raptor programs, and partners with Fanuc Robotics.
Rydev is headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia, founded in 2020, with a headcount of 11-50 employees.
Rydev operates as an ASIC design house for semiconductors with teams in Costa Rica and Argentina, and offers training workshops to bridge technical gaps.
Whitepath Fab Tech is headquartered in Ellijay, Georgia, founded in 1989, with a headcount of 201-500 employees (approx. 92).
Whitepath Fab Tech specializes in electrical distribution systems, wire harnesses, and control box assemblies as part of Electrical Components International.
Absolics Inc. is headquartered in Covington, Georgia, founded in 2021, with a headcount of 51-200 employees (approx. 82).
Absolics Inc. specializes in advanced packaging and glass substrate solutions for high-performance computing, data centers, and AI, and received a $100 million grant in January 2025.
Absolics Inc. is an affiliate of SKC, part of the SK Group.
Genesis Technology USA is headquartered in Norcross, Georgia, founded in 1994, with a headcount of 501-1000 employees.
Genesis Technology USA also known as Genesis Connected Solutions specializes in high-speed connectors and cables including SFP, QSFP, SlimSAS, and USB connectors.
World Micro Inc is headquartered in Roswell, Georgia, founded in 1994, with a headcount of 11-50 employees (approx. 67).
World Micro Inc maintains an average quality rating of 99.9% and serves Defense, Aerospace, and Commercial & Industrial sectors with operations in the United States, Mexico, and Malaysia.
Naeco, LLC is headquartered in Dunwoody, Georgia, founded in 1999, with a headcount of 11-50 employees (approx. 37).
Naeco, LLC holds ISO 9001, ISO 13485, and AS9100D certifications and received a $78,906 grant in March 2024 for R&D efforts.
Flight Display Systems is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, founded in 1999, with a headcount of 51-200 employees.
Flight Display Systems also known as ArgonFDS specializes in rugged display solutions and computing systems designed for military applications across air, land, and sea platforms.
Blue Bird Corporation is headquartered in Macon, Georgia, USA.
The company operates a manufacturing and assembly facility in Fort Valley, Georgia.
Blue Bird plans to build a new 600,000 square-foot Type D and EV production facility in Fort Valley, Georgia.
The company was awarded an $80 million MESC grant by the Department of Energy (DOE) to develop the new Fort Valley facility.
The total investment for the new Fort Valley plant is $160 million, with Blue Bird funding 50% of the costs.
Construction of the new Georgia facility is expected to take about two years, with a launch targeted for the end of 2026 or early 2027.
Final negotiations with the DOE regarding the grant are expected to conclude by December 2024.
Once operational, the new plant will increase Blue Bird's total production capacity to approximately 14,000 buses per year.
The new facility is expected to deliver an internal rate of return (IRR) of 28% with a payback period of two years post-launch.
Blue Bird aims to increase electric school bus sales to 1,150 units by FY2025.
Blue Bird targets annual electric school bus sales of 5,000 units in the period after FY2027.
Currently, more than 60% of Blue Bird's unit sales are non-diesel.
The company formed Clean Bus Solutions, a 50/50 joint venture with Generate Capital, to accelerate electric school bus adoption.
The Clean Bus Solutions JV offers customers a monthly service fee covering the Blue Bird electric bus fleet and charging infrastructure.
As of June 2024, commitments for electric school buses in the US reached 12,164 units.
The EPA's Clean School Bus Program provided over 67% of all committed electric school buses in the US.
In 2022, the EPA program allocated $900 million to fund nearly 2,300 electric buses across 365 school districts.
A second funding round in January 2024 added funding for 2,700 electric buses in 270 districts.
A third funding round awarded $900 million to support the replacement of diesel buses with 3,177 electric buses in nearly 500 districts.
The Bipartisan Infrastructure Law of 2021 authorizes the EPA to provide rebates and grants for replacing school buses with clean or zero-emission models, securing $5 billion over five years.
The 2023 EPA CSB Round 3 Rebate Program allocated $940 million for about 3,600 buses.
The 2024 Clean Heavy-Duty Vehicles Program, funded through the Inflation Reduction Act, will provide around $650 million for school buses.
Blue Bird expects to secure around 30% of orders for Type A, C, and D electric buses from federal funding initiatives.
Electric school bus commitments now span 49 states.
Approximately 200,000 students nationwide currently benefit from electric school bus transportation.
In Q3 FY2024, Blue Bird delivered over 200 electric buses, marking a nearly 40% year-over-year increase.
Strong demand from the EPA’s Clean School Bus Program drove a backlog of over 560 EV orders as of Q3 FY2024.
Buses funded by Phase 1 of the $5 billion program need to be delivered by the end of 2026.
Blue Bird manufactures Type C, Type D, and specialty school buses with passenger capacities ranging from 10 to 90.
The company's bus models offer various body styles and engine options, including alternative power and electric configurations.
Blue Bird generates approximately 93% of its sales in the US, ~7% in Canada, and less than 1% in the rest of the world.
Three major manufacturers dominate the US school bus industry: Blue Bird Corporation, IC Bus LLC, and Thomas Built Buses Inc., each holding roughly a 30% market share.
Lion Electric Co. operates as a niche manufacturer of zero-emission vehicles with approximately a 5% market share.
The company relies on specialist suppliers, some single-source, for critical components, creating supply chain vulnerability.
Blue Bird imports some components from China and other foreign countries, which may be subject to US trade policies and tariffs.
source_title: Investment analysis of BLUE BIRD CORP | Freedom24

facts: Auto industry in Alabama contributes about 10 percent of the state’s gross state product, according to Auburn University at Montgomery economist M. Keivan Deravi.
More than $18 billion has been pumped into Alabama from the state’s automotive industry, which includes three major assembly plants and many large suppliers and engine manufacturers.
In the last year, parts suppliers in Alabama announced projects worth $924 million in investments and more than 2,500 jobs.
Toyota’s engine plant in Huntsville marks its 15th anniversary, celebrated in August.
The Huntsville facility is the only Toyota engine plant worldwide that produces V-8, V-6, and 4-cylinder engines under one roof.
The plant houses about 1,350 workers in more than 1 million square feet of space.
Toyota has invested nearly $1 billion in the Huntsville plant, which produces almost 3,000 engines a day.
TVA certified a 1,252-acre megasite in Huntsville, Limestone County, Ala., meeting criteria including interstate access and rail service.
TVA has certified several megasites over the past 12 years, with five sites securing large tenants such as Volkswagen in Chattanooga.
Hyundai began mass production of the Santa Fe SUV at its Montgomery plant in the summer.
The Santa Fe model was previously manufactured at the Kia plant in West Point, Ga.
The Santa Fe joins the Sonata and Elantra sedan models at the Alabama assembly plant.
German supplier Gerhardi Kunststofftechnik announced in the summer it will build a new facility in Montgomery with a $37.9 million investment generating 235 new jobs.
Fehrer Automotive is investing $12 million in its Gadsden plant, adding more than 150 jobs and a new hot cure foaming line for foam products for Mercedes-Benz, Volkswagen, and Ford.
Korean parts supplier Seohan, which supplies Hyundai and Kia, is adding jobs in Auburn with a $9.5 million investment in new equipment; no firm job count was provided at the time of writing.
Lear Operations is expanding its Vance plant to make seats for the Mercedes C-Class, GLE, and GLS SUVs, creating 500 jobs.

Germany-based Eissmann Automotive is investing $14.5 million in its Pell City facility, building a new 130,000-square-foot addition for a production line that will house 200 workers; the company supplies Mercedes, Audi, VW, Porsche, and Tesla.

Mercedes-Benz is relocating its New Jersey engineering division to Jacksonville, where it also operates a 500,000-square-foot facility at the International Trade Port containing Southern region sales operations and a parts distribution center; the new unit will create 50 jobs.

Chinese tire manufacturer Sentury Tire Americas selected a site near LaGrange, Ga., for its first U.S. manufacturing plant after narrowing its search to the Memphis Regional Megasite in Haywood County, Tenn., and the LaGrange site.
The Sentury LaGrange project is valued at $500 million, will generate 600 jobs initially, and could see investment rise to as much as $1 billion.
German manufacturer Nesper International broke ground recently on a $1.5 million expansion at its LaGrange facility to manufacture plastic and hybrid parts, creating 12 jobs.

Germany-based Voestalpine Automotive Body Parts will spend $50 million to expand operations in Bartow County, creating 150 jobs; it currently operates a 100-employee plant about six miles north of Cartersville making metal car body parts.
Toyota’s Georgetown, Ky. plant tied with the Lexus facility in Kyushu, Japan for the fewest manufacturing defects worldwide according to J.D. Power.
Toyota has been assembling Lexus models in Kentucky, the only place outside Japan, for more than a year.

The Toyota Camry built in Georgetown, Ky., was named the most American-made car by Cars.com’s 2016 American-made index, which ranks cars based on the highest percentage of domestic parts used in assembly.

Toyota celebrated 30 years of car assembly in Georgetown, Ky. during the summer quarter; the plant has produced over 10 million cars in that time, houses 8,000 workers, and carries a $1.9 billion payroll in Kentucky.
India-based Rane Ltd., a manufacturer of high-pressure aluminum steering and compressor components, is investing $5.8 million in new equipment and tooling at its Russellville, Ky. plant.
American Howa Kentucky is investing over $7 million in its Bowling Green plant, creating 44 new jobs paying an average of $18 per hour.
Sumitomo Electric Wiring Systems will open a fifth Kentucky plant in Franklin, moving into an existing 65,000-square-foot facility that will house 145 workers.

Japan-based Kobelco Aluminum Products & Extrusions, a subsidiary of Kobe Steel, began construction over the summer on a new Bowling Green facility to make aluminum bumper beams and frames for the automotive industry.

Dr. Schneider Automotive Systems opened an expanded plant in Russell Springs, Ky., doubling its workforce to 300; the company makes vent systems for automakers and has spent $43 million on the facility since announcing it in August 2013.

Lowndes County, Miss., is now home to a third TVA-certified megasite, the Infinity Megasite, located west of the Golden Triangle Regional Airport near Columbus, Miss., alongside the Lowndes County and Crossroads Megasites.
The 1,144-acre Mississippi megasite features 100 megawatts of electricity, major water capacity, and is community-owned.

Mississippi State’s National Strategic Planning and Analysis Research Center reported Nissan’s Canton plant contributed $2.9 billion in direct and indirect measures, up from $2.5 billion in 2013, with local and state tax revenue rising from $180 million in 2013 to $300 million last year.

The 1,800-acre Chatham-Siler City Advanced Manufacturing Site received certification from the North Carolina Department of Commerce’s certified sites program, meaning most engineering and infrastructure work is complete and it is shovel-ready; it is the largest certified site in North Carolina.

The Greensboro-Randolph Megasite was certified by KPMG after passing a lengthy infrastructure and site readiness evaluation; the 1,480-acre Randolph County site is located about 30 miles south of Greensboro.
Japanese tire maker Yokohama Tire is consolidating its research and development to International Business Park in Concord, N.C., with a $2.7 million project creating 56 jobs.

Volvo exported over 170,000 cars last year from its Gothenburg, Sweden plant, doubling exports over the last year; this surge indicates brand recovery and bodes well for Volvo’s new plant in Berkeley County, S.C.
In late summer, Volvo posted its first job openings for the new $500 million Berkeley County plant; the site received 24,000 visits in two days before crashing on the third day due to high traffic.

Mercedes-Benz Vans broke ground in the summer quarter on a new $500 million van plant in North Charleston, S.C., located next to a facility where Mercedes reassembles Sprinter vans imported disassembled from Germany to avoid high duties.
The new 1 million-square-foot Mercedes-Benz Vans facility will house 1,300 workers when completed.
Honda is expanding its ATV and SxS vehicle plant in Florence County, S.C., with a $45 million project creating 250 new jobs.

Yanfeng Automotive Interiors, the world’s largest supplier of automotive interiors, is making a $71 million investment in a new Laurens County, S.C. plant to supply interior components to the nearby BMW plant, creating 35 initial jobs.

Chinese auto supplier JGBR is locating a new plant in Colleton County, S.C., investing $7 million in a Walterboro facility and hiring 65 workers to manufacture steel balls and ball bearings for the automotive industry.

Brose North America signed a lease on a 77,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Spartanburg, S.C., with a $6 million investment creating more than 60 new jobs to produce door systems and cooling air supply control systems for automotive customers.
Michigan-based International Mold Corp. is building a new plant in Greer, S.C., to supply floor molding and rim tooling equipment to the nearby BMW plant, creating 110 jobs.

Germany’s Evonik Industries will invest $129.4 million to build a Berkeley County, S.C. plant producing chemicals used in tire manufacturing; more tires are made in South Carolina than any other state, and Evonik will hire about 50 workers.
Michigan-based Tower International announced in the summer it will invest $75 million to establish its first South Carolina plant in Fountain Inn to produce metal components and assemblies.

Pure Power Technologies is expanding its Blythewood, S.C. operations with a $15 million project creating 79 new jobs in Richland County; the company makes new and re-manufactured diesel fuel injectors for automakers.
Auto parts manufacturer BASF is investing up to $60 million at its Seneca, S.C. plant to upgrade equipment for producing a new product used in heavy diesel engines.

Volkswagen AG is spending hundreds of millions of dollars and adding half a million square feet of factory space at its Chattanooga, Tenn. assembly plant to produce a second vehicle, a seven-seat SUV, while hiring 2,000 additional workers.

VW officials in Wolfsburg, Germany are allowing the company’s U.S. unit (headquartered in Virginia) to name the new SUV, requesting a distinct American name that fits the region; exported SUVs from Chattanooga will assume a different name.

The 1.5 million-square-foot Hankook Tire plant in Clarksville, Tenn., is nearing completion as an $800 million project housing up to 1,800 workers at full production with a capacity of 16,000 tires a day; the South Korean company also moved its North American headquarters to nearby Nashville.

Bridgestone is adding 450 jobs in Nashville and signed a decade-long office lease in Antioch, in addition to 1,700 employees moving into the company’s new headquarters being built in Nashville’s SoBro community.

Tennessee Gov. Bill Haslam stated in the summer quarter he has strong hopes that Volkswagen’s Chattanooga plant will receive some or all of $2 billion for zero-emissions technology from VW’s settlement with car owners, states, and the federal government following the diesel emissions scandal.
As part of the settlement, VW agreed to a $2.7 billion environmental remediation fund, a $2 billion investment in U.S. zero-emission vehicle initiatives, and will pay approximately $15 billion total.
Clean air and ride performance products manufacturer Tenneco is locating a new plant in Spring Hill, Tenn., to serve the local GM plant, expected to create 175 jobs over three years.

Japanese auto parts supplier Denso Manufacturing Tennessee broke ground on a $400 million expansion of its Maryville, Tenn. plant, adding space and 500 new jobs; the company makes parts for several automakers including Nissan in Tennessee.

Chattanooga Seating Systems, a joint venture of Magna Seating and Hollingsworth Logistics that manufactures and supplies seats for Volkswagen Chattanooga, will invest $5.4 million to expand its Hamilton County facility, creating 190 jobs.

Automotive parts supplier TBDN is investing $18.8 million in its Jackson, Tenn. facility; the joint venture between DENSO and Toyota Boshoku makes oil and air filters and filter housings and will create 35 new jobs in Madison County.
Telos Global will invest $32 million in a new Caryville, Tenn. plant, creating 63 new jobs.
Spanish parts supplier Gestamp is expanding its Chattanooga plant to make parts for Volkswagen’s new SUV, calling for 115 new workers.

Eurotranciatura USA, a joint venture between Japan-based Kuroda Precision Industries and Italy-based Euro Group S.p.A., is expanding in Paris, Tenn., with an investment of almost $13 million creating 170 new jobs to produce steel lamination products for generators and electric motors.

Toyota is relocating its entire headquarters from Torrance, Calif., to Plano, Texas, with up to 4,000 employees potentially moving; the primary driver is overall costs, particularly housing, with average Torrance homes in the low $700,000s versus high $200,000s in Plano.

The Greater Sacramento Area Economic Council is now targeting companies relocating to Dallas-Fort Worth and Austin from California as an alternative to Texas, pitching that Sacramento operating costs are similar to those in Texas.

Barcelona-based car parts manufacturer Sanjo Corte Fina will locate its North American headquarters and plant in Virginia Beach, Va., serving as a supplier to Stihl and automotive suppliers with a $17.5 million project creating 85 jobs.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Summer 2016 | Southern Business and Development

entity_name: Southern Business & Development Big Buffalo Manufacturing Announcements
canonical_name: Southern Business & Development Big Buffalo Manufacturing Announcements
facts: The article profiles 25 of the biggest manufacturing announcements made in the South from 1992-2017 by Southern Business & Development.
BMW’s Spartanburg County, S.C. plant paved the way for many more foreign automakers in the Southern Automotive Corridor, including Mercedes-Benz the next year.
Nissan Powertrain in Decherd, Tenn. began supplying electric motors to support U.S. production of the all-electric Nissan LEAF assembled in Tennessee in 2013.
Bridgestone's expansion in Aiken County, S.C., transformed Western South Carolina including Augusta, Ga.
Kia Motors announced it would build its first U.S. plant near the Alabama-Georgia border in West Point, Ga., a little over 10 years ago.
The first vehicle at the Kia West Point plant, a Kia Sorento, rolled off the line in November of 2009.
Kia and Hyundai plants share dozens of suppliers located in Alabama and Georgia due to a symbiotic relationship.
Kia’s engines are built by Hyundai in Alabama and Hyundai’s transmissions are built by Kia in Georgia.
During the economic recovery, both the Kia West Point and Hyundai Montgomery plants ran three shifts a day for a while.
A Kia executive found the 2,200-acre site in West Point, Ga., while driving to the Hyundai plant in Montgomery from Atlanta.
SB&D publisher Michael Randle identified Troup County, Ga., as the first location in Georgia on an Interstate 85 map between Montgomery and Georgia during Kia’s negotiations with Mississippi.
Randle contacted Jane Fryer, the economic development director in Troup County at the time, to initiate contact with Kia.
The Kia West Point plant transformed much of West Georgia and East Alabama, particularly Troup, Meriwether, and Harris counties in Georgia.

DENSO Manufacturing Athens Tennessee Inc. manufactures ignition and exhaust systems and gasoline delivery systems for global customers including Hyundai, GM, Ford, Honda, and Nissan, operating 18 facilities in the Southern Auto Corridor.
Volkswagen’s Chattanooga plant produces a sedan and a large SUV with plans to add a small SUV, and some of Northwest Georgia has benefited from its economic impact.
Alabama captured Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama with a $253 million incentive package, just edging out Kentucky for the automaker's first U.S. assembly facility.
Toyota site teams initially wanted to place a new plant somewhere in the traditional Southern Auto Corridor, which is spined by Interstate 65, before marketing pushed for Texas.
source_title: Big Buffalo Awards | Southern Business and Development

facts: FREYR Battery is a developer of clean battery solutions that provided insights into its strategic progress and financial standing during its Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2023 Conference Call.
The company underscored robust demand for batteries driven by a surge in electric vehicle (EV) sales and energy storage requirements.
FREYR reported a net loss of $24 million or $0.17 per share for the quarter ended December 31, 2023.
The company is preparing for fully automated battery production for customer testing in the first half of 2024.
The FREYR 2.0 growth initiative targets over 100 gigawatt hours of installed capacity across five distinct project opportunities.
FREYR is pursuing an ongoing Department of Energy (DoE) Title 17 loan application process, aiming for a conditional commitment by the end of 2024.
The company reported significant year-over-year growth in EV sales and an expanding energy storage system market.
FREYR is focused on industrializing its U.S.-based technology at the Giga America project in Georgia.
The company maintains a two-year cash runway with $276 million in reserves following December 2023 restructuring efforts.
FREYR is capitalizing on Inflation Reduction Act benefits for its Giga America project.
EV sales showed 69% growth year-over-year from January 2023 to January 2024, surpassing 1 million cars in January for the first time ever.
The energy storage system market in 2023 closed above 100 gigawatt hours per year for the first time, twice as high as initial estimates.
FREYR has proven its capability to produce electrochemically active electrodes through fully automated production of anodes and cathodes using next-generation 24M technology.
The Commercial Qualification Pilot (CQP) facility is 94% complete.
In February, the CQP was brought into operating mode on dry room conditions and began producing automated electrodes with active electrolyte slurry.
Production of sample battery cells for key customers at the CQP is FREYR’s number one priority for the first half of 2024.
The CQP serves as the means to achieve technical validation and broad customer acceptance of the 24M semi-solid product family.
Following customer testing, FREYR will move on to off-tech conversions with key customers to trigger the next wave of capital formation.

The company is focusing on five distinct project opportunities across the U.S. and Europe for semi-solid and conventional technology applications, encompassing more than 100 gigawatt hours of potential capacity.
FREYR currently owns 368 acres in Coweta County, Georgia, for its Giga America project.
The Georgia site is positioned to develop hundreds of jobs while attracting multiple billions of dollars of capital investment in the state.
FREYR is advancing its Title 17 application in deep coordination with the DoE Loans Program Office and re-energizing project level equity conversations for the second half of the year.
The conventional technology track is expected to be the first opportunity for production on the Coweta County site, with FREYR nearing a decision on which technology provider to select.
Technology selection for the Georgia site will be shared in the coming weeks, followed quickly by project finance and equity funding.
Project level equity is likely to be secured during the second half of the year, targeting start-up production in 2026 utilizing conventional technology.
FREYR successfully re-domiciled from Luxembourg to the United States as of December 31, 2023, with its main U.S. office located in Newnan, Georgia, near the Coweta County site.

The re-domicile aims to expand equity index inclusion potential, historically holding only ~3% of shares by index funds compared to a peer average over 20%, with potential to drive incremental holdings up to 45% of market capitalization.
For the full year ended December 31, 2023, FREYR reported a net loss of $72 million or $0.51 per share, compared to a loss of $99 million in the prior year.
The company incurred a $6 million one-time severance charge during the December restructuring, with most payouts expected in the first quarter.
By the end of the first quarter, employee headcount is expected to be reduced by 22% compared to November 2023, and consultants and project support personnel will be reduced by 26%.
FREYR ended 2023 with total assets of $732 million, net PP&E of $366 million, total liabilities of $97 million, no debt, and total shareholders’ equity of $635 million.
Total cash spending during 2023 was $287 million, coming in under budget due to a reduced scope of Giga Arctic spending plans.

The company is not forecasting significant capital expenditures for Giga Arctic in 2024 while working with the Norwegian Government on incentive programs to compete with the U.S. IRA and EU's TCTF programs.

Primary areas of focus for 2024 include fully automated battery cell production at the CQP using 24M technology, delivering testable cells to customers, continued development of Giga America, and pursuing a conventional partnership.
H1 2024 priorities include demonstrating semi-solid technology at Giga scale at the CQP and finalizing an agreement for the conventional technology track.
FREYR targets customer validation of its semi-solid process and product performance characteristics in the second half of the year to trigger offtake conversions.

facts: Kamtec announced in June it will open its first U.S. plant in Auburn, Ala., to make components for engine control and electric management in hybrid vehicles, with a $9.2 million investment creating 50 new jobs.

Georgia-based engineering and manufacturing company Yancey Engineered Solutions will invest $5.7 million in a new manufacturing facility in Cordele, Ga., creating 300 new jobs over the next several years in Crisp County for power generation packaging systems.

ArcelorMittal Building Solutions will establish its North American headquarters and a new manufacturing facility in Macon-Bibb County, Ga., with an initial investment bringing roughly $57 million and up to 70 new jobs, plus plans for an additional $50 million in investment and as many as 70 more jobs over the next several years.

Ford is targeting another billion-dollar-plus investment at its Louisville truck plant in Kentucky, with a rumored $2 billion expansion on 70 acres of land adjacent to the existing plant that assembles the Ford Super Duty, the Ford Expedition and Lincoln Navigator models, which already houses roughly 9,000 workers.

Faith Technologies will invest $80.5 million in a new electric components plant in Ouachita Parish, La., creating 200 jobs in a 500,000-square-foot facility that will use modular methods to produce electric assemblies for the clean energy industry.

NovaSpark Energy is investing $30 million to establish a new facility in Houma, La., scaling production of its atmospheric hydrogen generators used in defense and energy applications to meet increasing national demand for diverse energy solutions, creating 300 jobs across West Monroe and Houma.

USA Rare Earth will invest approximately $1.2 billion in Cherokee County, S.C., at Bailey Park in Blacksburg, to produce neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) permanent magnets and refined rare earth metals, creating about 490 new jobs in the state.
Mosdorfer is investing $44.55 million for its first U.S. operation in Oconee County, S.C., manufacturing hardware for high voltage transmission lines and energy infrastructure, creating 107 new jobs.

Tesla’s megapack battery factory going up in Brookshire, Texas, is expected to open this year to produce 50 GWh of energy storage annually, which is more than the company’s California and China plants combined, and will house 1,500 workers by 2028.
SEG Solar is building a new 4.6-gigawatt solar module plant in the Houston metro area, featuring a 1.1 million-square-foot factory and a large warehouse.
Toyo Solar is locating a $357 million solar cell manufacturing facility in the Houston metro, which will house about 400 workers.
TerraFlow Energy is building a new battery plant and headquarters in the Houston market of Katy, producing batteries that can power about 10,000 homes for one hour and have a life of 10 hours.

Blue Energy has applied for Texas incentives to build a $6.24 billion hybrid power plant near Victoria, Texas, combining natural gas turbines and small modular nuclear reactors to scale faster than a nuclear-only plant.
Florida-based NextEra Energy announced plans to acquire Virginia’s Dominion Energy in an all-stock deal to form a super energy giant amid soaring power demand from the A.I. data center build-out.
